source: ESV Global Study Bible (Crossway) — Used under license

notes.text: Several speakers explain how the beast and the kings desert the prostitute.
Fallen, fallen. Compare Isa. 21:9. Babylon’s fall will turn the great city into a wilderness inhabited by every unclean spirit, bird, and beast.

The prophets often asked the OT Israelites to come out of (be different than) the cultures where they were exiled (Isa. 52:11; Jer. 51:6–9, 45). Such separation was equally relevant to the NT church and it remains so today (2 Cor. 6:14–18; 1 Pet. 2:11–12).

God is just. He will pay (Babylon) back as she herself has paid back (see Ex. 21:23–25). The double portion (see Isa. 40:2; Jer. 16:18) of punishment is for the violence she inflicted on the saints (Rev. 19:2).
Babylon’s boast, I sit as a queen, I am no widow will be silenced in a single day (compare Isa. 47:7–9).
Laments come from kings, merchants, and sailors who profited from Babylon’s power and wealth.
The prostitute symbolizes all corrupt economic systems. Even human souls are reduced to cargo. They are traded as slaves to provide cheap labor for greedy merchants.
Everyone in heaven (saints and apostles and prophets) rejoice in God’s judgment of Babylon (compare 12:12).
God will judge Babylon and the beast for shedding the blood of his prophets and saints (see 6:10; 11:8; 17:6).
